questions.q: The parametric representation of a point on the ellipse whose foci are $(-1,0)$ and $(7,0)$ and eccentricity $1 / 2$, is
questions.solution:
Distance between two foci, $2 a e=7+1=8$
$\therefore$
$$
a e=4
$$
$$
\Rightarrow
$$
$a=8$
$$
\left(\because e=\frac{1}{2} \text { given }\right)
$$
Now,
$$
\begin{array}{l}
b^{2}=a^{2}\left(1-e^{2}\right)=64\left(1-\frac{1}{4}\right) \\
b^{2}=48 \Rightarrow b=4 \sqrt{3}
\end{array}
$$
$\therefore$
Since, the centre of the ellipse is the mid point of the line joining two foci, therefore the coordinates of the centre are $(3,0)$. $\therefore$ Its equation is
$$
\frac{(x-3)^{2}}{8^{2}}+\frac{(y-0)^{2}}{(4 \sqrt{3})^{2}}=1
$$
Hence, the parametric coordinates of a point on Eq. (i) are $(3+8 \cos \theta, 4 \sqrt{3} \sin \theta)$.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

The eccentricity of the ellipse is
questions.solution:
Let, 4,2=S1 and 2,2=S2 and eccentricity of ellipse is e
then S1S2=2ae and PS1+PS2=2a
(where 2a is length of major axis)
⇒e=S1S2PS1+PS2=222+2
⇒e=12+1=2-1=tanπ8

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: If the lines $2 x-y+3=0$ and $4 x+k y+3=0$ are conjugate with respect to the ellipse $5 x^2+6 y^2-15=0$, then $k$ equals
questions.solution:
$2 x-y+3=0$ and $4 x+k y+3=0$ are
conjugate with respect to ellipse $5 x^2+6 y^2-15=0$
We know that $a_1 x+b_1 y+c_1=0$ and
$a_2 x+b_2 y+c_2=0$ are conjugate with $\frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b}=1$
Then, $a^2 a_1 a_2+b^2 b_1 b_2=c_1 c_2$
$\therefore$ Here, $a_1=2, b_1=-1, c_1=3$
$\begin{aligned} & a_2=4, b_2=k, c_2=3 \\ & a^2=\frac{15}{5}, b^2=\frac{15}{6}\end{aligned}$
$\therefore \frac{15}{5}(2)(4)-\left(\frac{15}{6}\right)(k)=9$
$\Rightarrow \quad k=6$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics

questions.q: Let P be a point on the ellipse x2a2+y2b2=1, 0<b<a. Let the line parallel to y-axis passing through P meets the circle x2+y2=a2 at the point Q such that P and Q are on the same side of x-axis. For two positive real numbers r and s, find the locus of the point R on PQ such that PR:RQ=r:s, as P varies over the ellipse.
questions.solution:
Given, PRRQ=rs

⇒  α-bsinθasinθ-α=rs

⇒  αs-bsinθs=rasinθ-αr

⇒  αs+αr=rasinθ+bsinθ·s

⇒  αs+r=sinθra+bs

⇒  α=sinθra+bsr+s

Let the coordinate of R be h,k.

⇒ h=acosθ

and k=α=ar+bssinθr+s

⇒  cosθ=ha, sinθ=kr+sar+bs

On squaring and adding, we get

sin2θ+cos2θ=h2a2+k2r+s2ar+bs2

⇒ 1=h2a2+k2r+s2ar+bs2

Hence, locus of R is x2a2+y2r+s2ar+bs2=1
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.q: Suppose <math><mi>S</mi></math> and <math><mi>S</mi><mi>′</mi></math> are foci of the ellipse <math><mfrac><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mrow><mn>25</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mrow><mn>16</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo></math> If <math><mi>P</mi></math> is a variable point on the ellipse and if <math><mo>∆</mo></math> is the area (in sq. units) of the triangle <math><mi>P</mi><mi>S</mi><mi>S</mi><mi>′</mi></math>, then the maximum value of <math><mo>∆</mo></math> is double of
questions.solution:
Given , a2=25 and b2=16

∴ e=1-b2a2=1-1625= 35

So, the coordinates of foci S and S′ are (3, 0) and (-3, 0) respectively.

Let, P5cos⁡θ,4sin⁡θ be a variable point on the ellipse.

Then, ∆=area of ∆PSS′=12301-3015cos⁡θ4sin⁡θ1=12sin⁡θ            

Since the value of sin⁡θ lies between -1 and 1

So, the maximum value of area of ∆PSS′ is 12 sq. units

Also, a8+b4≥2a4b2 (since, A.M≥G.M) 

⇒3a8+b4a4b2≥6

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The coordinates of any point, in the parametric form, on the ellipse whose foci are $(-2,0),(8,0)$ and eccentricity $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$, is
questions.solution:
The foci are $(-2,0)$ and $(8,0)$. The distance between the foci is $2 a e=10$ $$ \begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow a e=5 \\ \because \quad e=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \text { (given) } \\ \therefore a=5 \sqrt{2} \\ \text { Now }, b^2=a^2\left(1-e^2\right) \\ \Rightarrow \quad b^2=50\left(1-\frac{1}{2}\right)=25 \\ \Rightarrow \quad b=5 \end{array} $$ The centre of the ellipse is the mid-point of the line joining two foci. Therefore, the coordinates of the centre is $(3,0)$.
Thus, the parametric coordinates of a point on Eq. (i) are $(3+5 \sqrt{2} \cos \theta, 5 \sin \theta)$.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.q: If <math><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> is a point on the ellipse <math><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> then the area of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">S</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">′</mi><mo>=</mo></math>
questions.solution:
x2a2 + y2b2=1⇒ y12b2=1-x12a2

y1=baa2-x12

ΔSPS′=12× 2ae × y1=ae × baa2-x12

ΔSPS′=bea2-x12

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: If $F_1$ and $F_2$ are the focii of the ellipse $16 x^2+25 y^2=400$ and $P$ is any point on it, then the value of the product $P F_1 \cdot P F_2$ lie in the interval
questions.solution:
Given, equation of ellipse $16 x^2+25 y^2=400$ $\Rightarrow \frac{x^2}{25}+\frac{y^2}{16}=1$, Comparing to $\frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1$ Then, $a=5$ and $b=4$ Let $P(5 \cos \theta, 4 \sin \theta)$ be any point on the ellipse.
Then, $$ \begin{aligned} & P F_1=5+5 e \cos \theta \\ & P F_2=5-5 e \cos \theta \end{aligned} $$ Now, $$ \begin{aligned} & P F_1 \cdot P F_2=25-25 e^2 \cos ^2 \theta \\ & =25-25 \times \frac{9}{25} \times \cos ^2 \theta \\ & =25 \sin ^2 \theta+25 \cos ^2 \theta-9 \cos ^2 \theta \\ & =25 \sin ^2 \theta+16 \cos ^2 \theta \\ & =16+9 \sin ^2 \theta=f(\theta) \text { say } \\ & \because \quad 0 \leq \sin ^2 \theta \leq 1 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 0 \leq 9 \sin ^2 \theta \leq 9 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 16 \leq 16+9 \sin ^2 \theta \leq 25 \\ & \therefore \quad 16 \leq f(\theta) \leq 25 \\ & \end{aligned} $$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
If $\mathrm{B}$ is end point of minor axis of the ellipse $b^{2} x^{2}+a^{2} y^{2}=a^{2} b^{2}(a>b)$ and $\mathrm{S}$ and
S' are foci of ellipse such that $\Delta \mathrm{SBS}^{\prime}$ is an equilateral triangle, then eccentricity $\mathrm{e}=$
questions.solution: Correct option is A Given $\mathrm{S}(-\mathrm{ae}, 0), \mathrm{T}(\mathrm{ae}, 0), \mathrm{B}(0, \mathrm{~b})$ As STB is an equilateral triangle In $\triangle \mathrm{TOB}$ $\tan 60^{\circ}=\frac{\mathrm{OB}}{\mathrm{OS}}$ $\sqrt{3}=\frac{\mathrm{OB}}{\mathrm{OS}}=\frac{\mathrm{b}}{\mathrm{ae}}$ $(\sqrt{3} a e)^{2}=b^{2}$ $3 a^{2}\left(1-\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}\right)=b^{2}$ $3\left(1-\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}\right)=\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}$ $\frac{3}{4}=\frac{b^{2}}{a^{2}}$ $e=\sqrt{1-\frac{3}{4}}=\frac{1}{2}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: A point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> moves on <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo></math>plane such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mi>S</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>P</mi><msup><mi>S</mi><mo>'</mo></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>K</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>S</mi><mo>'</mo></msup><mo>(</mo><mo>-</mo><mi>K</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo><mo>,</mo></math> then which of the following is not true about the locus of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo> </mo><mo>?</mo></math>
questions.solution:
Given, S(K, 0) and S'(-K, 0), 

Now, SS'=|2K| and PS+PS'=4 (given)

Now for the ellipse we know that,

PS+PS'>SS'

⇒4>|2K|

⇒K∈(-2, 2).

If P,S & S' are collinear, then

PS+PS'=SS'

⇒K=±2.

If PS+PS'<SS', then no locus exists.

⇒K∈(-∞, -2)∪(2, ∞).
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The equation of the ellipse having a vertex at $(6,1)$, a focus at $(4,1)$ and the eccentricity $\frac{3}{5}$ is
questions.solution:
Let the equation of ellipse is,
$$
\frac{(x-h)^2}{a^2}+\frac{(y-k)^2}{b^2}=1
$$
Now,
$$
\begin{array}{rlrl}
a-a e & =2 \\
\Rightarrow & a\left(1-\frac{3}{5}\right) & =2 \Rightarrow a=5 \\
& \text { So, } & b & =4
\end{array}
$$
So,
Now, vertex comparing the vertex, we are getting
$$
\text { and } \quad \begin{aligned}
& 6-h=5 \Rightarrow h=1 \\
& 1-k=0 \Rightarrow k=1
\end{aligned}
$$
So, equation of required ellipse is
$$
\frac{(x-1)^2}{25}+\frac{(y-1)^2}{16}=1 .
$$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: If the line $2 x-3 y+4=0$ cuts the ellipse $x=3 \cos \theta$, $y=5 \sin \theta$ in $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ and $(\alpha, \beta)$ is the midpoint of $\overline{\mathrm{AB}}$, then $3 \beta-2 \alpha=$
questions.solution:
Given line $2 x-3 y+4=0$ cuts the ellipse $x=3 \cos$ $\theta, y=5 \sin \theta$.
Here, $a=3, b=5$.
Required equation of ellipse is
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1 \\
& \frac{x^2}{9}+\frac{y^2}{25}=1 . .
\end{aligned}
$$
Satisfy the line $3 y=2 x+4$
$$
\mathrm{y}=\frac{2 \mathrm{x}+4}{3}
$$
From (i)
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 25 \mathrm{x}^2+9 \mathrm{y}^2=225 \\
& 25 \mathrm{x}^2+9\left(\frac{2 \mathrm{x}+4}{3}\right)^2=225 \\
& 25 \mathrm{x}^2+\frac{9}{9}\left(4 \mathrm{x}^2+16+16 \mathrm{x}\right)=225 \\
& 29 \mathrm{x}^2+16 \mathrm{x}-209=0
\end{aligned}
$$
Let $\mathrm{A}$ is $\left(\mathrm{x}_1, \mathrm{y}_1\right)$ and $\mathrm{B}\left(\mathrm{x}_2, \mathrm{y}_2\right)$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{x}_1+\mathrm{x}_2=\frac{-16}{29} \\
& \frac{\mathrm{x}_1+\mathrm{x}_2}{2}=\frac{-8}{29}
\end{aligned}
$$
Here, $(\alpha, \beta)$ is the mid point of the line $\mathrm{AB}$
$$
\alpha=\frac{-8}{29}
$$
Now, $2 x=3 y-4 \Rightarrow x=\frac{3 y-4}{2}$
From (i)
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{25}{4}\left(16+9 y^2-24 y\right)+9 y^2=225 \\
& 225 y^2+36 y^2-600 y+400=900
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
261 y^2-600 y-500=0
$$
So, $\mathrm{y}_1+\mathrm{y}_2=\frac{600}{261}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{\mathrm{y}_1+\mathrm{y}_2}{2}=\frac{300}{261} \\
& \beta=\frac{300}{261}
\end{aligned}
$$
Now, $3 \beta-2 \alpha=3 \times \frac{300}{261}+2 \times \frac{8}{29}$
$$
=\frac{300}{87}+\frac{16}{29}=\frac{300+48}{87}=\frac{348}{87}=4
$$
So, option (b) is correct.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: If the latusrectum of an ellipse subtends a right angle at the centre of that ellipse, then the eccentricity of that ellipse is
questions.solution:
Let equation of ellipse $\frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1$
Let $L L^{\prime}$ is latusrectum, then co-ordinate of $$ L\left(a e, \frac{b^2}{a}\right) $$ $L L^{\prime}$ subtend $\pi / 2$ Angle at the centre, so angle $$ L C S=\pi / 4 $$ $$ \begin{array}{rlrl} \Rightarrow & \tan \frac{\pi}{4} & =\frac{\frac{b^2}{a}}{a e} \\ \Rightarrow & 1 & =\frac{b^2}{a^2 e} \\ \Rightarrow & a^2 e & =b^2=a^2\left(1-e^2\right) \\ \Rightarrow & e & =1-e^2 \\ \Rightarrow & e^2+e-1 & =0 \\ \Rightarrow & & e & =\frac{-1 \pm \sqrt{5}}{2} \Rightarrow e=\frac{\sqrt{5}-1}{2} . \end{array} $$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: An ellipse having the coordinate axes as its axes and its major axis along $Y$-axis, passes through the point $(-3,1)$ and has eccentricity $\sqrt{\frac{2}{5}}$. Then its equation is
questions.solution:
Let the equation of ellipse is
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1 \\
& e=\sqrt{\frac{2}{5}} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \sqrt{\frac{a^2-b^2}{a^2}}=\sqrt{\frac{2}{5}} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \frac{a^2-b^2}{a^2}=\frac{2}{5} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad 5 a^2-5 b^2=2 a^2 \\
&
\end{aligned}
$$
Given,
$$
\begin{array}{ll}
\Rightarrow & 3 a^2=5 b^2 \\
\Rightarrow & a^2=\frac{5 b^2}{3}
\end{array}
$$
Now, ellipse passes through $(-3,1)$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \frac{(-3)^2}{a^2}+\frac{(1)^2}{b^2}=1 \Rightarrow 9 b^2+a^2=a^2 b^2 \\
& \Rightarrow 9 b^2+\frac{5 b^2}{3}=\frac{5 b^4}{3} \Rightarrow \frac{32 b^2}{3}=\frac{5 b^4}{3} \\
& \Rightarrow b^2=\frac{32}{5}
\end{aligned}
$$
From Eq. (i), we get
$$
\begin{array}{rlrl}
\Rightarrow & & \frac{x^2}{32}+\frac{y^2}{\frac{32}{3}} & =1 \\
\Rightarrow & 3 x^2+5 y^2 & =32 \\
\Rightarrow & & 3 x^2+5 y^2-32 & =0 .
\end{array}
$$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.q: The length of the latus rectum of an ellipse is <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>18</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>5</mn></mrow></mfrac><mi></mi></math> and eccentricity is <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>5</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>,</mo></math> then equation of the ellipse is…

⇒b2=95a        …(i)
and, e=1-b2a2=45⇒1-b2a2=1625
⇒b2a2=1-1625=925
⇒95aa2=925⇒95a=925
⇒a=5
∴b2=955=9 [using Eq. (i)]
∴ Required equation of ellipse,
x225+y29=1

questions.solution:

B=a-62 9+b-524≤1 

For set A, 

For corner coordinates of the set A is 4,4,4,6,6,4,6,3. 

For set B, 

Now, Put 4,4 in the equation of the set B, 

49+14≤1, 4,4 lies in the set B.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>S</mi><mo>'</mo></msup></math> are the focii of an ellipse, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> is one end of the minor axis and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>∠</mo><mi>S</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>S</mi><mo>'</mo><mo>=</mo><mn>90</mn><mo>°</mo></math>, then the eccentricity of that ellipse is
questions.solution:
Let the coordinates of the focii of an ellipses are Sae,0 and S'-ae,0.

Since, ∠SBS'=90

∴SS'2=SB2+S'B2

2ae2=ae-02+0-b22+-ae-02+0-b22

⇒2ae2=ae2+b2+ae2+b22

⇒4a2e2=2a2e2+2b2

⇒2a2e2=2b2

⇒b2a2=e2  ...i

Also,

e2=1-b2a2

⇒e2=1-e2; Using i

⇒e=12.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: If the eccentricity and the length of the latus rectum of an ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>b</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> respectively, then the sum of the lengths of major axis and minor axis of the ellipse is
questions.solution:
Given,

Equation of ellipse x2a2+y2b2=1, eccentricity e=32 and length of latusrectum 2b2a=1 ...........1

Now we know that formula of eccentricity b2=a21-e2 so by using this we get b2a2=1-34=14 ........2

Now by solving equation 1 & 2 we get,

a=2 & b=1

So sum of major axis and minor axis will be 4+2=6.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The focus and corresponding directrix of an ellipse are <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>3,4</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> respectively. If the eccentricity of the ellipse is <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></math>, then the coordinates of the centre of the ellipse are
questions.solution:
Let, the lengths of the semi-major axis and semi-minor axis of the ellipse are a & b respectively, then the distance of the focus 3,4 from the corresponding directrix x+y-1=0 is equal to ae-ae
⇒a12-a12=3+4-12=32
⇒3a2=32⇒a=22
Distance between the focus and centre =ae=2
The slope of the axis of the ellipse is 1=tan⁡θ
⇒cos⁡θ,sin⁡θ=12,12 or -12,-12
The points on the axis of the ellipse at a distance 2 units from 3,4 are 3±212,4±212
⇒ Points are 2,3 or 4,5 , but the centre is 4,5 because it is far from the directrix as compared to 2,3.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The lines $y=2 x+\sqrt{76}$ and $2 y+x=8$ touch the ellipse $\frac{x^2}{16}+\frac{y^2}{12}=1$. If the point of intersection of these two lines lie on a circle. whose centre coincides with the centre of that ellipse, then the equation of that circle is
questions.solution:
Given, equation of tangents to the ellipse are,
$$
\begin{aligned}
& y=2 x+\sqrt{76} \text { and } 2 y+x=8 \Rightarrow y=-\frac{1}{2} x+4 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad m_1=2 \text { and } m_2=-- \\
& \because \quad \text { Here, } m_1 \times m_2=-1
\end{aligned}
$$
It means that the given tangents are perpendicular to each other.

Thus, required circle will be the director circle to ellipse. Hence, required equation of circle is
$$
\begin{aligned}
x^2+y^2 & =a^2+b^2=16+12 \\
\Rightarrow \quad x^2+y^2 & =28
\end{aligned}
$$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: If <math><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi></math> touches the ellipse <math><mn>3</mn><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>12</mn></math> at the point <math><mi>P</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then the value of the length <math><mi>O</mi><mi>P</mi></math> (where <math><mi>O</mi></math> is the origin) is equal to
questions.solution:
If y=x+c touches x24+y23=1

then, c=±41+3

=±7

Let's assume, c=7  and y=x+7 touches x24+y23=1 at h,k.

then, xh4+yk3=1 is same as y-x=7

⇒h4-1=k31=17

⇒h,k=-47,37=P

Hence, OP=167+97=57 units
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: If $\frac{\pi}{3}, \theta$ are the eccentric angles of the ends of a focal chord of the ellipse $\frac{x^2}{16}+\frac{y^2}{12}=1$, then $\tan \theta=$
questions.solution:
Given ellipse, $\frac{x^2}{16}+\frac{y^2}{12}=1$
Let eccentricity of ellipse be ' $e$ ' Then
$b^2=a^2\left(1-e^2\right)$
Here, $\quad b^2=12, a^2=16$
$\therefore \quad 12=16\left(1-e^2\right)$
$1-e^2=\frac{3}{4}$
or $e^2=1-\frac{3}{4}=\frac{1}{4} \text { or } e=\frac{1}{2}$
If $\alpha, \beta$ are the eccentric angles of the ends of a focal chord of the ellipse, then eccentricity is given by
$e=\frac{\cos \left(\frac{\alpha-\beta}{2}\right)}{\cos \left(\frac{\alpha+\beta}{2}\right)}$
Here, $\alpha=\frac{\pi}{3}, \beta=\theta$, and $e=\frac{1}{2}$
$\frac{1}{2}=\frac{\cos \left(\frac{\pi / 3-\theta}{2}\right)}{\cos \left(\frac{\pi / 3+\theta}{2}\right)}$
Multiplying in $N^r$ and $D^r$ by $2 \sin \left(\frac{\pi / 3+\theta}{2}\right)$
$\frac{1}{2}=\frac{2 \sin \left(\frac{\pi / 3+\theta}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{\pi / 3-\theta}{2}\right)}{2 \sin \left(\frac{\pi / 3+\theta}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{\pi / 3+\theta}{2}\right)}$
$=\frac{\sin \left(\frac{\pi / 3+\theta+\pi / 3-\theta}{2}\right)+\sin \left(\frac{\pi / 3+\theta-\pi / 3+\theta}{2}\right)}{\sin (\pi / 3+\theta)}$
$\left\{\because 2 \sin A \cos B=\sin \left(\frac{A+B}{2}\right)+\sin \left(\frac{A-B}{2}\right)\right.$ and $2 \sin A \cos A=\sin 2 A\}$
$\frac{1}{2}=\frac{\sin \frac{\pi}{3}+\sin \theta}{\sin \frac{\pi}{3} \cos \theta+\cos \frac{\pi}{3} \sin \theta}$
$\sin \frac{\pi}{3} \cos \theta+\cos \frac{\pi}{3} \sin \theta=2\left(\sin \frac{\pi}{3}+\sin \theta\right)$
$\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cos \theta+\frac{1}{2} \sin \theta=2\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\sin \theta\right)$
$\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cos \theta+\frac{1}{2} \sin \theta=\sqrt{3}+2 \sin \theta$
or $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \cos \theta=\frac{3}{2} \sin \theta+\sqrt{3}$
$\cos \theta=\sqrt{3} \sin \theta+2$ or $\frac{1}{2} \cos \theta-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \sin \theta=1$
or $\quad \cos \frac{\pi}{3} \cos \theta-\sin \frac{\pi}{3} \sin \theta=1$
or $\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3}+\theta\right)=1$ or $\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3}+\theta\right)=\cos 0^{\circ}$
On comparing both sides, we get
$\frac{\pi}{3}+\theta=0^{\circ} \text { or } \theta=-\frac{\pi}{3} \quad \therefore \quad \tan \theta=\tan \left(\frac{-\pi}{3}\right)$
$\tan \theta=-\sqrt{3}$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mi>D</mi></math> are conjugate semi-diameters of the ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>b</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>, then, the locus of the mid-point of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mi>D</mi></math> is
questions.solution:
Let (h,k) be the midpoint of PD.

Then, h=acosθ-asinθ2

⇒2h=acosθ-asinθ

⇒2ha=cosθ-sinθ  ...1

And, k=bsinθ+bcosθ2

⇒2k=bsinθ+bcosθ

⇒2kb=sinθ+cosθ  ...2

Squaring and adding 1 and 2, we get

4h2a2+4kkb2=cosθ-sinθ2+sinθ+cosθ2

⇒4h2a2+4k2b2=2

⇒h2a2+k2b2=12

Hence, the locus of (h,k) is

x2a2+y2b2=12
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q:
Assertion (A): The image of $\frac{x^2}{25}+\frac{y^2}{16}=1$ in the line $x+y=10$ is
$$
\frac{(x-10)^2}{16}+\frac{(y-10)^2}{25}=1 \text {. }
$$
Reason (R): The image of a curve ' $\mathrm{C}$ ' in a line $\mathrm{L}$ is the locus of the image of every point of $\mathrm{C}$ with respect to the line $\mathrm{L}$.
The correct option among the following is:
options: (A) is true, (R) is true and (R) is the correct explanation for (A)
(A) is true, (R) is true but (R) is not the correct explanation for (A)
questions.solution:
Given equation of ellipse is $\frac{x^2}{25}+\frac{y^2}{16}=1$
The image of the ellipse in the line $x+y-10$ will be $\frac{(x-h)^2}{16}+\frac{(y-k)^2}{25}=1$
Centre of original equation is $(0,0)$ and other equation is $(h, k)$
Midpoint is $\left(\frac{h}{2}, \frac{k}{2}\right)$
Then, $x+y-10=0$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{h}{2}+\frac{k}{2}=10 \\
& h+k=20
\end{aligned}
$$
Now, Given equation is perpendicular to the line joining the coordinates $(0,0) \&(h, k)$
So, $\frac{k}{h} \times(-1)=(-1)$
From (i),
$$
h+k=20
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 2 h=20 \\
& h=10
\end{aligned}
$$
Then, $k=10$
Equation of ellipse $=\frac{(x-10)^2}{16}+\frac{(y-10)^2}{25}=1$
It is obtained by the image of every point on the equation of ellipse in the line $x+y-10=0$.
Therefore, reason is also true.
So, option (a) is correct.
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: The length of the latus rectum of the curve represented by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>cos</mi><mo>⁡</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>sin</mi><mo>⁡</mo><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>cos</mi><mo>⁡</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>sin</mi><mo>⁡</mo><mi>t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> is
questions.solution:
x3=cos⁡t+sin⁡t and y4=cos⁡t-sin⁡t
x32+y42=2
x2322+y422=1
Hence, the length of the latus rectum =232242=92
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: If $a x^2+b y^2=15$ is the equation of the ellipse for which distance between its foci is 2 and distance between its directrices is 5 , then $a+b=$
questions.solution:
Given equation is $a x^2+b y^2=15$
$$
\frac{x^2}{\left(\sqrt{\frac{15}{a}}\right)^2}+\frac{y^2}{\left(\sqrt{\frac{15}{b}}\right)^2}=1
$$
Now, $a^{\prime}=\sqrt{\frac{15}{a}}, b^{\prime}=\sqrt{\frac{15}{b}}$
According to question,
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 2 d e=2 \Rightarrow a^{\prime} e=1 \\
& \frac{2 a^{\prime}}{e}=5 \Rightarrow \frac{a^{\prime}}{e}=\frac{5}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$
Multiply (ii) and (iii),
$$
a^{\prime} e \times \frac{a^{\prime}}{e}=1 \times \frac{5}{2}
$$
$$
a^{\prime 2}=\frac{5}{2} a^{\prime}= \pm \frac{\sqrt{5}}{\sqrt{2}}
$$
from (i)
$$
a^{\prime} e=1
$$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& e= \pm \frac{\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{5}} \\
& b^{\prime 2}=a^{\prime 2}=\left(1-e^2\right) \\
& b^{\prime 2}=\frac{5}{2}\left(1-\frac{2}{5}\right)=\frac{5}{2} \times \frac{3}{5}=\frac{3}{2} \\
& b^{\prime}= \pm \frac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$
Now, compare $a^{\prime} \& b^{\prime}$ values,
$$
\frac{\sqrt{15}}{\sqrt{a}}= \pm \frac{\sqrt{5}}{\sqrt{2}}
$$
square both sides,
$$
\frac{15}{a}=\frac{5}{2} \Rightarrow a=6 \text {. }
$$
Similarly, $\frac{\sqrt{15}}{\sqrt{b}}= \pm \frac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{2}}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{15}{b}=\frac{3}{2} \Rightarrow b=10 \\
& \text { So, } a+b=10+6=16
\end{aligned}
$$
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: Let <math><mi>P</mi></math> and <math><mi>Q</mi></math> be two points on the curves <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math> and <math><mfrac><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mrow><mn>8</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> respectively. Then the minimum value of the length <math><mi>P</mi><mi>Q</mi></math> is
questions.solution:
The shortest distance PQ occurs along the common normal;
i.e., when P is 0,2 & Q is 0,2.
Hence PQminimum=2-2
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.examName: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
questions.q: From the focus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> of the ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mn>45</mn></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mn>20</mn></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>, a ray of light is sent which makes an angle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced separators="|"><mfrac><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><msqrt><mn>5</mn></msqrt></mfrac></mfenced></math> with the positive direction of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis, upon reaching the ellipse surface, the ray is reflected from it. Slope of the reflected ray is
questions.solution:
Ellipse: x245+y220=1

Foci: ±5, 0

Let's say θ=cos-1-15⇒cosθ=-15 ⇒tanθ=-2

Now, equation of line passing through -5, 0 and of slope -2 can be written as y=-2x+5or y=-2x-10

Now, if we solve this line with given ellipse equation

x245+-2x-10220=1

This gives us point of intersection above x-axis at -6, 2. (Point -3,4 will be rejected as slope of line joining it with focus will be 2 but slope of line given is  -2).

Now, since line meets the ellipse above x-axis at -6, 2. Therefore, it is also the point from where ray got reflected.

From reflection property of ellipse, we can say that reflected ray passes through the other focus whose coordinates are  5, 0.

Hence, slope =2-0-6-5=-211
questions.source: 99 Percentile Qs Bank for JEE Main
